content.text: The MCP ecosystem contains hundreds of ready-to-use servers covering file systems, databases, developer tools, communication platforms, cloud services, and more. Rather than building every tool from scratch, agents can compose existing servers through
configuration and connect to them with a standard

attrs.alt: MCP server ecosystem: official servers from @modelcontextprotocol (filesystem, GitHub, PostgreSQL, Slack, Fetch, Memory), community servers (Brave Search, Puppeteer, Sentry, Linear, AWS KB), and installation via pip/npx/Docker

content.text: The MCP server repository (github.com/modelcontextprotocol/servers) is the canonical starting point. Each subdirectory is a separate installable server with its own README, tools list, and configuration examples.
Anthropic maintains a set of reference servers in the
repository. These are the most thoroughly documented and are kept in sync with protocol changes.
query (read-only SQL), list tables, describe schema
create_entities, create_relations, search_nodes — persistent knowledge graph
for Claude Desktop). The host reads this file at startup, spawns each server subprocess, and makes all their tools available.
In an agent, connect to installed servers using
exactly as you would for a custom server. The tools are identical to what the

import asyncio
from mcp import ClientSession, StdioServerParameters
from mcp.client.stdio import stdio_client

# Connect to the official GitHub MCP server
github_params = StdioServerParameters(
    command="npx",
    args=["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-github"],
    env={"GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN": "ghp_..."},
)

async def main():
    async with stdio_client(github_params) as (r, w):
        async with ClientSession(r, w) as session:
            await session.initialize()
            tools = await session.list_tools()
            print("GitHub tools:", [t.name for t in tools.tools])

            # List issues in a repository
            result = await session.call_tool(
                "list_issues",
                {"owner": "anthropics", "repo": "anthropic-sdk-python", "state": "open"},
            )
            print(result.content[0].text)

asyncio.run(main())

import asyncio, os
from mcp import ClientSession, StdioServerParameters
from mcp.client.stdio import stdio_client

# Restrict access to a specific directory — passed as a CLI arg
fs_params = StdioServerParameters(
    command="npx",
    args=["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem", "/tmp/workspace"],
)

async def main():
    async with stdio_client(fs_params) as (r, w):
        async with ClientSession(r, w) as session:
            await session.initialize()

            # Write a file
            await session.call_tool("write_file", {
                "path": "/tmp/workspace/hello.txt",
                "content": "Hello from MCP!",
            })

            # Read it back
            result = await session.call_tool("read_file", {
                "path": "/tmp/workspace/hello.txt",
            })
            print(result.content[0].text)   # Hello from MCP!

asyncio.run(main())
Beyond the official repository, the community maintains thousands of additional servers:
— searchable registry of 1000+ community MCP servers. Each listing shows the tools available, install command, and usage examples.
— curated GitHub repository listing servers by category (databases, productivity, developer tools, AI/ML, cloud providers).
— many servers are published with the
Evaluating a Server Before Using It
An MCP server runs as a subprocess with the same privileges as your agent process. Before adding a community server, check:
— does it request broader credentials than its tools justify? A search server should not need write access.
— credentials should be passed as environment variables, never in tool arguments.
— prefer servers with open source code you can audit over binary-only distributions.
packages or servers from established companies (AWS Labs, Stripe, Sentry) carry lower risk than anonymous uploads.
Composing Multiple Servers in an Agent

Real agents aggregate tools from several servers simultaneously. The following example wires together the GitHub server, the filesystem server, and the Fetch server to build a tool-enabled code review agent.
) to prevent collisions when multiple servers expose similarly named tools. The model sees prefixed names; the dispatch layer strips the prefix to call the right session.
Different servers suit different task profiles. This guide maps common agent task types to the servers that address them:
When a server fails to start or returns unexpected results, a few diagnostic steps isolate the problem quickly:

import asyncio, sys
from mcp import ClientSession, StdioServerParameters
from mcp.client.stdio import stdio_client

async def diagnose_server(params: StdioServerParameters):
    """Print server info and tool list, exit on any failure."""
    print(f"Connecting: {params.command} {' '.join(params.args)}")
    try:
        async with stdio_client(params) as (r, w):
            async with ClientSession(r, w) as session:
                init = await session.initialize()
                print(f"Server: {init.serverInfo.name} v{init.serverInfo.version}")
                print(f"Protocol: {init.protocolVersion}")
                tools = await session.list_tools()
                for t in tools.tools:
                    print(f"  tool: {t.name} — {t.description}")
                print(f"Total tools: {len(tools.tools)}")
    except Exception as exc:
        print(f"FAILED: {exc}", file=sys.stderr)
        raise

params = StdioServerParameters(
    command="npx",
    args=["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-github"],
    env={"GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN": "ghp_yourtoken"},
)
asyncio.run(diagnose_server(params))

— the server is producing non-JSON output on stdout. Check for print statements or logging going to stdout instead of stderr.
. It typically contains the underlying API error (rate limit, permission denied, 404).

) and changing the command to the binary name.
always pulls the latest version, which can introduce breaking changes. In production, pin to a specific version to keep agent behavior deterministic across deployments.
. Check the server's changelog before upgrading — new tool names, removed parameters, or changed defaults all affect your agent's prompts.
In CI/CD pipelines, list server versions in a
file alongside your Python dependencies. Install them during the pipeline's setup step so the container image is self-contained and
downloads are not needed at runtime:

Discover community servers via Smithery, awesome-mcp-servers, npm, PyPI, and GitHub Topics.
Before trusting a community server, audit scope, credential handling, source availability, and maintainer reputation.
